title: Dippa
text: A from-scratch Python rebuild of published Warren-Averbach and (modified) Williamson-Hall peak-profile methods, currently in pre-alpha: the packaging and I/O scaffold exists, the fitting and analysis logic does not yet.

title: Dippa
text:
Status
This site describes a project in progress, not a released tool. Nothing here has been validated for real analysis. See the source audit for what has actually been inspected in the original MATLAB codebase, and the roadmap for what’s deliberately excluded from v0.1 and why.

title: Dippa
text:
Origin
Dippa rebuilds ThomasHSimm/DPPA, a MATLAB GUI tool developed during and after a PhD in materials science, implementing methods from:

T.H. Simm, P.J. Withers, J. Quinta da Fonseca, An evaluation of diffraction peak profile analysis (DPPA) methods to study plastically deformed metals, Materials & Design 111 (2016) 331–343. doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2016.08.091
T.H. Simm, P.J. Withers, J. Quinta da Fonseca, Peak broadening anisotropy in deformed face-centred cubic and hexagonal close-packed alloys, J. Appl. Cryst. 47(5) (2014) 1535–1551. doi:10.1107/S1600576714015751







NoteWhy not just keep using the MATLAB version?





The original tool works and remains available, unchanged, at ThomasHSimm/DPPA. It has no automated tests, mixes fitting logic with GUI callbacks, depends on the current working directory for file locations, and implements sample-vs-instrument and cubic-vs-HCP handling as separate copy-pasted code paths rather than as parameters. Dippa’s goal is a package where the scientific core is callable and testable independent of any GUI, matching the original’s published methods but not its implementation structure.

title: Source audit
text: This audit is based on reading the actual dippa_v3.zip source pulled from ThomasHSimm/DPPA, plus loading its bundled .mat reference files, rather than on the original repository’s documentation or memory of the codebase.

title: Source audit
text:
Inventory
197 files: 80 .m, 34 .mat, 55 .png, 15 .html, 3 .fig, 2 .pl (UDF format converters), plus example data files. Two entry points match the original README’s documented workflow — BIGdippa.m (165 KB, peak fitting) then dippaFC.m (85 KB, Fourier/Williamson-Hall analysis on BIGdippa’s output). 34 of the 80 .m files build file paths from cd (MATLAB’s current directory), which is why the app can’t currently be scripted or tested headlessly without changes.

title: Source audit
text:
The aa / aabcg parameter arrays
Loaded directly from the bundled 0. variables/fit.mat:








Variable
Shape
Meaning




aa
(6, 11)
10 peaks (columns 0–9) + 1 shared background column (column 10, quadratic: c0 + c1·x + c2·x²). 6 rows because this example uses the asymmetric peak variant.


aabcg
(2, 10)
Per-peak local background offset/slope, separate from the shared background in aa.


aa_I / aabcg_I
same shapes
Same structure, for the instrumental-standard fit — stored in a completely separate file (fit_I.mat), confirming sample and instrument code paths are duplicated rather than parameterised.









NoteGlossary: peak model parameters






Position, intensity, width, shape — the four core pseudo-Voigt parameters per peak (pk_voigt2.m).
Asymmetric variant — a 6-parameter peak shape (pk_alpha_asymm.m) used when peaks aren’t symmetric about their centre; adds a second width/shape pair.
Kα1/Kα2 doublet — X-ray tubes emit two close wavelengths; pk_alpha.m fits them as one peak with a fixed intensity/position relationship derived from the tube’s known wavelengths, rather than as two independent peaks.

title: Source audit
text:
Profile function and known limitation
pv_tv_aa.m sums pseudo-Voigt peaks plus a shared quadratic background. Peak shape and the Kα1/Kα2 doublet are handled in pk_alpha.m, which hardcodes wavelengths for three tubes only:



Tube
λα1 (Å)
λα2 (Å)




Cu
1.54056
1.54439


Co
1.789010
1.792900


Fe
1.93604
1.93998



Anything else falls through with no explicit error. A port should take wavelength as an explicit input, with these three pairs as one built-in table among others, rather than reproducing the tube-name lookup as the only path.

title: Source audit
text:
Williamson-Hall implementation
getWH.m implements modified Williamson-Hall (contrast-factor weighted), matching the standard Ungár/Borbély formulation:
C = Aest * (1 - q*H2)
X = g .* sqrt(C)
fit to one of three selectable forms — linear (size + strain·X), root-sum-square (sqrt(size² + strain²·X²)), or quadratic-in-X (size + strain·X²). This is a known, citable method, not an idiosyncratic one — useful confirmation for anyone deciding whether a Python port is worth trusting.

title: Source audit
text:
Two independent duplication axes
Beyond the sample/instrument duplication above, six files are _HCP-suffixed near-copies of their cubic-symmetry counterparts (getWH_HCP.m, contrastHCP.m, and four others). Symmetry handling and sample/instrument handling are each implemented as separate copy-pasted branches. Representing both as data rather than as which function you call removes both axes in one design decision — likely the single highest-value structural change a port makes over the original.

title: Source audit
text:
Reproducibility of this audit
Anyone can repeat this: dippa_v3.zip is public and MIT-licensed at ThomasHSimm/DPPA. The .mat inspection used scipy.io.loadmat on the bundled 0. variables/fit.mat and genset.mat. No proprietary or unpublished data was used.
The full write-up, including MATLAB global-vs-preference-store inconsistencies and a short landscape check, is in AUDIT.md in the repository root.

title: Roadmap
text: The biggest risk flagged during scoping was building everything at once — full CMWP-equivalent scope, all symmetries, GUI, and packaging in one pass. This page exists to make the narrower scope explicit and keep it that way.

title: Roadmap
text:
Not yet started
No fitting or analysis logic has been ported. Current state is: packaging scaffold, CI, and a loader for the legacy .mat state files (I/O only, no fitting math). See the source audit for what’s been inspected.

title: Roadmap
text:
Proposed v0.1 scope

Cubic symmetry only (no HCP).
Sample fitting only (no instrumental-correction UI in the core package).
Pseudo-Voigt peak fitting matching the original pv_tv_aa / pk_alpha behaviour, including Kα1/Kα2 doublet handling for Cu/Co/Fe.
Modified Williamson-Hall analysis (getWH.m’s three variants).
One worked example from the original tool’s bundled reference data, reproduced within a documented numerical tolerance.
No GUI.

title: Roadmap
text:
Deliberately excluded from v0.1
Each of these was excluded not because it’s hard, but because each one adds a code path that needs its own parity check against the original MATLAB output before it can be trusted:

Warren-Averbach Fourier analysis (dippaFC.m / FourierFunctions/).
HCP / anisotropic contrast factors.
Instrumental-standard deconvolution.
Any GUI (interactive fitting is real scientific-judgement territory — see the note below — but comes after the core is trustworthy on its own).
Anything resembling CMWP or Rietveld refinement.







NoteWhy a GUI at all, eventually





Peak-profile fitting isn’t always a fully automatic optimisation problem — choosing a fit range, deciding whether a peak overlaps another phase, judging whether a converged fit is physically credible are expert decisions the original GUI captured. The intended design keeps that judgement in an optional interactive layer that edits a config and calls the same core functions used programmatically, rather than reimplementing fitting math inside GUI callbacks the way the original does.

title: Roadmap
text:
Parity-testing approach
Before any fitting code is considered done, its output gets compared against the real .mat reference files bundled in the original repo (SS316_logINDI_RES.mat, SSnew_interpBCG_fit.mat, and the files under 4. RES/), not against synthetic data alone. This is slower than writing code first and checking later, deliberately — the goal is a package that reproduces known-good numbers, not one that merely runs.
